titleStmt.title: The Officer in Charge at New Delhi ( (Merrell) ) to the Secretary of State

div.head: The Officer in Charge at New Delhi ( Merrell ) to the Secretary of State

opener.dateline:
New Delhi , July 3, 1944—3 p.m.
[Received July 3—1:15 p.m.]

div.p: 485. Following from John Davies:John Paton Davies, Jr., Second Secretary of Embassy in China, on detail to General Stilwell.

“The Department has presumably been informed by the Embassy at Chungking that in response to representations from the Vice President, the Generalissimo has given permission for an observers group
from General Stilwell’s command to visit Chinese Communist territory.See memorandum of June 23 by the Second Secretary of Embassy in China, p. 460. I expect that two of the four Foreign Service officers detailed to General Stilwell will go in the first group: ServiceJohn Stewart Service, Second Secretary of Embassy in China. concentrating on Yenan and LuddenRaymond P. Ludden, Second Secretary of Embassy in China. concentrating on 18th Group HQ. After exploring Delhi and Chungking sources on internal political developments in Japan, EmmersonJohn K. Emmerson, Second Secretary of Embassy in China. expects to join observers group to tap Communist sources on same subject.

Information collected by these three officers will, of course, be given to Embassy and Department. While in North China these officers will naturally undertake possible special assignments which Embassy or Department may request directly or through Army.

Now comes the catch. Deputy Chief-of-Staff, Chungking, radios me urgently requesting replacement for Service at Chungking stipulating replacement should have Service’s qualifications else he would be burden rather than help. Only candidates coming to mind are PenfieldJames K. Penfield, Second Secretary of Embassy in China. or Sprouse,Philip D. Sprouse, Third Secretary of Embassy in China. Penfield, preferably, because of previous experience with Army.

With Ringwalt presumably quitting Kweilin, can he free Penfield or Sprouse for detail same basis as Service? Later Service and Penfield or Sprouse can rotate Yenan, Chungking to mutual advantage of Embassy and Army. Reluctant as Ambassador may be to see another Foreign Service officer detailed to the Army I think he will agree that for some time to come the only way the Department can get its political observers into the growingly important Communist area is by detailing them to the Army.
Sent to Department, repeated to Embassy.”
